1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
  3â†’ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11â†’import kotlin.time.*
 12â†’import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.nanoseconds
 13â†’
 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A unit for measuring time.
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 18â†’ * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 21â†’ * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 22â†’ * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 23â†’ *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 26â†’ */
 27â†’@Serializable(with = DateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 28â†’public sealed class DateTimeUnit {
 29â†’
 30â†’    /** Produces a date-time unit that is a multiple of this unit times the specified integer [scalar] value. */
 31â†’    public abstract operator fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit
 32â†’
 33â†’    /**
 34â†’     * A date-time unit that has the precise time duration.
 35â†’     *
 36â†’     * Such units are independent of the time zone.
 37â†’     * Any such unit can be represented as some fixed number of nanoseconds.
 38â†’     */
 39â†’    @Serializable(with = TimeBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 40â†’    public class TimeBased(
 41â†’        /**
 42â†’         * The length of this unit in nanoseconds.
 43â†’         */
 44â†’        public val nanoseconds: Long
 45â†’    ) : DateTimeUnit() {
 46â†’        private val unitName: String
 47â†’        private val unitScale: Long
 48â†’
 49â†’        init {
 50â†’            require(nanoseconds > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $nanoseconds ns." }
 51â†’            // find a concise string representation for the unit with this duration
 52â†’            when {
 53â†’                nanoseconds % 3600_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 54â†’                    unitName = "HOUR"
 55â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 3600_000_000_000
 56â†’                }
 57â†’                nanoseconds % 60_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 58â†’                    unitName = "MINUTE"
 59â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 60_000_000_000
 60â†’                }
 61â†’                nanoseconds % 1_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 62â†’                    unitName = "SECOND"
 63â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000_000
 64â†’                }
 65â†’                nanoseconds % 1_000_000 == 0L -> {
 66â†’                    unitName = "MILLISECOND"
 67â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000
 68â†’                }
 69â†’                nanoseconds % 1_000 == 0L -> {
 70â†’                    unitName = "MICROSECOND"
 71â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000
 72â†’                }
 73â†’                else -> {
 74â†’                    unitName = "NANOSECOND"
 75â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds
 76â†’                }
 77â†’            }
 78â†’        }
 79â†’
 80â†’        override fun times(scalar: Int): TimeBased = TimeBased(safeMultiply(nanoseconds, scalar.toLong()))
 81â†’
 82â†’        /**
 83â†’         * The length of this unit as a [Duration].
 84â†’         */
 85â†’        public val duration: Duration
 86â†’            get() = nanoseconds.nanoseconds
 87â†’
 88â†’        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 89â†’            this === other || (other is TimeBased && this.nanoseconds == other.nanoseconds)
 90â†’
 91â†’        override fun hashCode(): Int = nanoseconds.toInt() xor (nanoseconds shr Int.SIZE_BITS).toInt()
 92â†’
 93â†’        override fun toString(): String = formatToString(unitScale, unitName)
 94â†’    }
 95â†’
 96â†’    /**
 97â†’     * A date-time unit equal to some number of days or months.
 98â†’     *
 99â†’     * Operations involving `DateBased` units are performed on dates. The same operations on [Instants][Instant]
100â†’     * require a [TimeZone] to find the corresponding [LocalDateTimes][LocalDateTime] first to perform
101â†’     * the operation with the date component of these `LocalDateTime` values.
102â†’     */
103â†’    @Serializable(with = DateBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
104â†’    public sealed class DateBased : DateTimeUnit() {
105â†’        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
106â†’        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.DayBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.DayBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
107â†’        public typealias DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased
108â†’        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
109â†’        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
110â†’        public typealias MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased
111â†’    }
112â†’
113â†’    /**
114â†’     * A date-time unit equal to some number of calendar days.
115â†’     *
116â†’     * A calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours, thus a `DayBased`-unit cannot be expressed as a multiple of some [TimeBased]-unit.
117â†’     *
118â†’     * The reason lies in time zone transitions, because of which some days can be 23 or 25 hours.
119â†’     * For example, we say that exactly a whole day has passed between `2019-10-27T02:59` and `2019-10-28T02:59`
120â†’     * in Berlin, despite the fact that the clocks were turned back one hour, so there are, in fact, 25 hours
121â†’     * between the two date-times.
122â†’     */
123â†’    @Serializable(with = DayBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
124â†’    public class DayBased(
125â†’        /**
126â†’         * The length of this unit in days.
127â†’         */
128â†’        public val days: Int
129â†’    ) : DateBased() {
130â†’        init {
131â†’            require(days > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $days days." }
132â†’        }
133â†’
134â†’        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased(safeMultiply(days, scalar))
135â†’
136â†’        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
137â†’            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.DayBased && this.days == other.days)
138â†’
139â†’        override fun hashCode(): Int = days xor 0x10000
140â†’
141â†’        override fun toString(): String = if (days % 7 == 0)
142â†’            formatToString(days / 7, "WEEK")
143â†’        else
144â†’            formatToString(days, "DAY")
145â†’    }
146â†’
147â†’    /**
148â†’     * A date-time unit equal to some number of months.
149â†’     *
150â†’     * Since different months have different number of days, a `MonthBased`-unit cannot be expressed a multiple of some [DayBased]-unit.
151â†’     */
152â†’    @Serializable(with = MonthBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
153â†’    public class MonthBased(
154â†’        /**
155â†’         * The length of this unit in months.
156â†’         */
157â†’        public val months: Int
158â†’    ) : DateBased() {
159â†’        init {
160â†’            require(months > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $months months." }
161â†’        }
162â†’
163â†’        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased(safeMultiply(months, scalar))
164â†’
165â†’        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
166â†’            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased && this.months == other.months)
167â†’
168â†’        override fun hashCode(): Int = months xor 0x20000
169â†’
170â†’        override fun toString(): String = when {
171â†’            months % 12_00 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12_00, "CENTURY")
172â†’            months % 12 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12, "YEAR")
173â†’            months % 3 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 3, "QUARTER")
174â†’            else -> formatToString(months, "MONTH")
175â†’        }
176â†’    }
177â†’
178â†’    protected fun formatToString(value: Int, unit: String): String = if (value == 1) unit else "$value-$unit"
179â†’    protected fun formatToString(value: Long, unit: String): String = if (value == 1L) unit else "$value-$unit"
180â†’
181â†’    public companion object {
182â†’        /**
183â†’         * A nanosecond, which is `1/1_000_000_000` of a second.
184â†’         */
185â†’        public val NANOSECOND: TimeBased = TimeBased(nanoseconds = 1)
186â†’
187â†’        /**
188â†’         * A microsecond, which is `1/1_000_000` of a second, or `1_000` nanoseconds.
189â†’         */
190â†’        public val MICROSECOND: TimeBased = NANOSECOND * 1000
191â†’
192â†’        /**
193â†’         * A millisecond, which is `1/1_000` of a second, or `1_000_000` nanoseconds.
194â†’         */
195â†’        public val MILLISECOND: TimeBased = MICROSECOND * 1000
196â†’
197â†’        /**
198â†’         * A second.
199â†’         */
200â†’        public val SECOND: TimeBased = MILLISECOND * 1000
201â†’
202â†’        /**
203â†’         * A minute, which is 60 seconds.
204â†’         */
205â†’        public val MINUTE: TimeBased = SECOND * 60
206â†’
207â†’        /**
208â†’         * An hour, which is 60 minutes, or 3600 seconds.
209â†’         */
210â†’        public val HOUR: TimeBased = MINUTE * 60
211â†’
212â†’        /**
213â†’         * A calendar day.
214â†’         *
215â†’         * Note that a calendar day is not the same as 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
216â†’         */
217â†’        public val DAY: DayBased = DayBased(days = 1)
218â†’
219â†’        /**
220â†’         * A week, which is 7 [calendar days][DAY].
221â†’         */
222â†’        public val WEEK: DayBased = DAY * 7
223â†’
224â†’        /**
225â†’         * A month.
226â†’         *
227â†’         * Note that a month doesn't have a constant number of calendar days in it.
228â†’         */
229â†’        public val MONTH: MonthBased = MonthBased(months = 1)
230â†’
231â†’        /**
232â†’         * A quarter, which is three [months][MONTH].
233â†’         */
234â†’        public val QUARTER: MonthBased = MONTH * 3
235â†’
236â†’        /**
237â†’         * A year, which is 12 [months][MONTH].
238â†’         */
239â†’        public val YEAR: MonthBased = MONTH * 12
240â†’
241â†’        /**
242â†’         * A century, which is 100 [years][YEAR], or 1200 [months][MONTH].
243â†’         */
244â†’        public val CENTURY: MonthBased = YEAR * 100
245â†’    }
246â†’}
247â†’

1â†’# kotlinx-datetime
  2â†’
  3â†’[![Kotlin Alpha](https://kotl.in/badges/alpha.svg)](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/components-stability.html)
  4â†’[![JetBrains official project](https://jb.gg/badges/official.svg)](https://confluence.jetbrains.com/display/ALL/JetBrains+on+GitHub) 
  5â†’[![GitHub license](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache%20License%202.0-blue.svg?style=flat)](http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
  6â†’[![Maven Central](https://img.shields.io/maven-central/v/org.jetbrains.kotlinx/kotlinx-datetime.svg?label=Maven%20Central)](https://search.maven.org/search?q=g:%22org.jetbrains.kotlinx%22%20AND%20a:%22kotlinx-datetime%22)
  7â†’[![Kotlin](https://img.shields.io/badge/kotlin-1.9.21-blue.svg?logo=kotlin)](http://kotlinlang.org)
  8â†’[![KDoc link](https://img.shields.io/badge/API_reference-KDoc-blue)](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/)
  9â†’[![Slack channel](https://img.shields.io/badge/chat-slack-blue.svg?logo=slack)](https://kotlinlang.slack.com/messages/kotlinx-datetime/)
 10â†’[![TeamCity build](https://img.shields.io/teamcity/build/s/KotlinTools_KotlinxDatetime_Build_All.svg?server=http%3A%2F%2Fteamcity.jetbrains.com)](https://teamcity.jetbrains.com/viewType.html?buildTypeId=KotlinTools_KotlinxDatetime_Build_All&guest=1)
 11â†’
 12â†’A multiplatform Kotlin library for working with date and time.
 13â†’
 14â†’See [Using in your projects](#using-in-your-projects) for the instructions how to setup a dependency in your project.
 15â†’
 16â†’## Design overview
 17â†’
 18â†’There are a few guiding principles in the design of `kotlinx-datetime`. First of all, it is pragmatic, focused
 19â†’on the most common problems developers face every day (pun intended) when working with dates and times. It is not
 20â†’all-encompassing and lacks some domain-specific utilities that special-purpose applications might need.
 21â†’We chose convenience over generality, so the API surface this library provides is as minimal as possible
 22â†’to meet the use-cases.
 23â†’
 24â†’The library puts a clear boundary between the physical time of an instant and the local, time-zone dependent 
 25â†’civil time, consisting of components such as year, month, etc that people use when talking about time. 
 26â†’We intentionally avoid entities in the library that mix both together and could be misused.
 27â†’However, there are convenience operations that take, for example, a physical instant and perform a calendar-based 
 28â†’adjustment (such as adding a month); all such operations
 29â†’explicitly take a time-zone information as parameter to clearly state that their result depends on the civil time-zone
 30â†’rules which are subject to change at any time.
 31â†’
 32â†’The library is based on the ISO 8601 international standard, other ways to represent dates and times are out of
 33â†’its scope. Internationalization (such as locale-specific month and day names) is out the scope, too. 
 34â†’
 35â†’## Types
 36â†’
 37â†’The library provides a basic set of types for working with date and time:
 38â†’
 39â†’- `Instant` to represent a moment on the UTC-SLS time scale;
 40â†’- `Clock` to obtain the current instant;
 41â†’- `LocalDateTime` to represent date and time components without a reference to the particular time zone; 
 42â†’- `LocalDate` to represent the components of date only;
 43â†’- `LocalTime` to represent the components of time only;
 44â†’- `TimeZone` and `FixedOffsetTimeZone` provide time zone information to convert between `Instant` and `LocalDateTime`;
 45â†’- `Month` and `DayOfWeek` enums;
 46â†’- `DateTimePeriod` to represent a difference between two instants decomposed into date and time units;
 47â†’- `DatePeriod` is a subclass of `DateTimePeriod` with zero time components,
 48â†’it represents a difference between two LocalDate values decomposed into date units.
 49â†’- `DateTimeUnit` provides a set of predefined date and time units to use in arithmetic operations on `Instant` and `LocalDate`. 
 50â†’- `UtcOffset` represents the amount of time the local date/time at a particular time zone differs from the date/time at UTC.
 51â†’
 52â†’### Type use-cases
 53â†’
 54â†’Here is some basic advice on how to choose which of the date-carrying types to use in what cases:
 55â†’
 56â†’- Use `Instant` to represent a timestamp of the event that had already happened in the past (like a timestamp of 
 57â†’  a log entry) or will definitely happen in a well-defined instant of time in the future not far away from now 
 58â†’  (like an order confirmation deadline in 1 hour from now).
 59â†’  
 60â†’- Use `LocalDateTime` to represent a time of the event that is scheduled to happen in the far future at a certain 
 61â†’  local time (like a scheduled meeting in a few months from now). You'll have to keep track of the `TimeZone` of 
 62â†’  the scheduled event separately. Try to avoid converting future events to `Instant` in advance, because time-zone 
 63â†’  rules might change unexpectedly in the future. In this [blog post](https://codeblog.jonskeet.uk/2019/03/27/storing-utc-is-not-a-silver-bullet/), you can read more about why it's not always 
 64â†’  a good idea to use `Instant` everywhere.
 65â†’  
 66â†’  Also, use `LocalDateTime` to decode an `Instant` to its local date-time components for display and UIs.
 67â†’  
 68â†’- Use `LocalDate` to represent the date of an event that does not have a specific time associated with it (like a birth date).
 69â†’
 70â†’- Use `LocalTime` to represent the time of an event that does not have a specific date associated with it.
 71â†’ 
 72â†’## Operations
 73â†’
 74â†’With the above types you can get the following operations done.
 75â†’
 76â†’
 77â†’### Getting the current moment of time
 78â†’
 79â†’The current moment of time can be captured with the `Instant` type. 
 80â†’To obtain an `Instant` corresponding to the current moment of time, 
 81â†’use `now()` function of the `Clock` interface:
 82â†’
 83â†’```kotlin
 84â†’val clock: Clock = ...
 85â†’val currentMoment = clock.now()
 86â†’```
 87â†’
 88â†’An instance of `Clock` can be injected through the function/class parameters, 
 89â†’or you can use its default implementation `Clock.System` that represents the system clock:
 90â†’
 91â†’```kotlin
 92â†’val currentMoment = Clock.System.now()
 93â†’```
 94â†’
 95â†’
 96â†’### Converting an instant to local date and time components
 97â†’
 98â†’An `Instant` is just a counter of high resolution time intervals since the beginning of time scale.
 99â†’To get human readable components from an `Instant` value, you need to convert it to the `LocalDateTime`
100â†’type that represents date and time components without a reference to the particular time zone.
101â†’
102â†’The `TimeZone` type provides the rules to convert instants from and to date/time components.
103â†’
104â†’```kotlin
105â†’val currentMoment: Instant = Clock.System.now()
106â†’val datetimeInUtc: LocalDateTime = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC)
107â†’val datetimeInSystemZone: LocalDateTime = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
108â†’```
109â†’
110â†’A `LocalDateTime` instance exposes familiar components of the Gregorian calendar: 
111â†’`year`, `month`, `dayOfMonth`, `hour`, and so on up to `nanosecond`.
112â†’The property `dayOfWeek` shows what weekday that date is,
113â†’and `dayOfYear` shows the day number since the beginning of a year.
114â†’
115â†’
116â†’Additional time zones can be acquired by their string identifier with the `TimeZone.of(id: String)` function.
117â†’```kotlin
118â†’val tzBerlin = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
119â†’val datetimeInBerlin = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(tzBerlin)
120â†’```
121â†’
122â†’A `LocalDateTime` instance can be constructed from individual components:
123â†’
124â†’```kotlin
125â†’val kotlinReleaseDateTime = LocalDateTime(2016, 2, 15, 16, 57, 0, 0)
126â†’```
127â†’
128â†’An instant can be obtained from `LocalDateTime` by interpreting it as a time moment
129â†’in a particular `TimeZone`:
130â†’
131â†’```kotlin
132â†’val kotlinReleaseInstant = kotlinReleaseDateTime.toInstant(TimeZone.of("UTC+3"))
133â†’```
134â†’
135â†’### Getting local date components
136â†’
137â†’A `LocalDate` represents a local date without time. You can obtain one from an `Instant`
138â†’by converting it to `LocalDateTime` and taking its `date` property.
139â†’
140â†’```kotlin
141â†’val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
142â†’val today: LocalDate = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).date
143â†’// or shorter
144â†’val today: LocalDate = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
145â†’```
146â†’Note, that today's date really depends on the time zone in which you're observing the current moment.
147â†’
148â†’`LocalDate` can be constructed from three components, year, month, and day:
149â†’```kotlin
150â†’val knownDate = LocalDate(2020, 2, 21)
151â†’```
152â†’
153â†’### Getting local time components
154â†’
155â†’A `LocalTime` represents local time without date. You can obtain one from an `Instant`
156â†’by converting it to `LocalDateTime` and taking its `time` property.
157â†’
158â†’```kotlin
159â†’val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
160â†’val thisTime: LocalTime = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).time
161â†’```
162â†’
163â†’A `LocalTime` can be constructed from four components, hour, minute, second and nanosecond:
164â†’```kotlin
165â†’val knownTime = LocalTime(hour = 23, minute = 59, second = 12)
166â†’val timeWithNanos = LocalTime(hour = 23, minute = 59, second = 12, nanosecond = 999)
167â†’val hourMinute = LocalTime(hour = 12, minute = 13)
168â†’```
169â†’
170â†’### Converting instant to and from unix time
171â†’
172â†’An `Instant` can be converted to a number of milliseconds since the Unix/POSIX epoch with the `toEpochMilliseconds()` function.
173â†’To convert back, use the companion object function `Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(Long)`.
174â†’
175â†’### Converting instant and local date/time to and from the ISO 8601 string
176â†’
177â†’`Instant`, `LocalDateTime`, `LocalDate` and `LocalTime` provide shortcuts for
178â†’parsing and formatting them using the extended ISO-8601 format.
179â†’The `toString()` function is used to convert the value to a string in that format, and 
180â†’the `parse` function in companion object is used to parse a string representation back. 
181â†’
182â†’```kotlin
183â†’val instantNow = Clock.System.now()
184â†’instantNow.toString()  // returns something like 2015-12-31T12:30:00Z
185â†’val instantBefore = Instant.parse("2010-06-01T22:19:44.475Z")
186â†’```
187â†’
188â†’`LocalDateTime` uses a similar format, but without `Z` UTC time zone designator in the end.
189â†’
190â†’`LocalDate` uses a format with just year, month, and date components, e.g. `2010-06-01`.
191â†’
192â†’`LocalTime` uses a format with just hour, minute, second and (if non-zero) nanosecond components, e.g. `12:01:03`.
193â†’
194â†’```kotlin
195â†’LocalDateTime.parse("2010-06-01T22:19:44")
196â†’LocalDate.parse("2010-06-01")
197â†’LocalTime.parse("12:01:03")
198â†’LocalTime.parse("12:00:03.999")
199â†’LocalTime.parse("12:0:03.999") // fails with an IllegalArgumentException
200â†’```
201â†’
202â†’### Working with other string formats
203â†’
204â†’When some data needs to be formatted in some format other than ISO-8601, one
205â†’can define their own format or use some of the predefined ones:
206â†’
207â†’```kotlin
208â†’// import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
209â†’
210â†’val dateFormat = LocalDate.Format {
211â†’    monthNumber(padding = Padding.SPACE)
212â†’    char('/')
213â†’    dayOfMonth()
214â†’    char(' ')
215â†’    year()
216â†’}
217â†’
218â†’val date = dateFormat.parse("12/24 2023")
219â†’println(date.format(LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC)) // "20231224"
220â†’```
221â†’
222â†’#### Using Unicode format strings (like `yyyy-MM-dd`)
223â†’
224â†’Given a constant format string like the ones used by Java's
225â†’[DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/format/DateTimeFormatter.html) can be
226â†’converted to Kotlin code using the following invocation:
227â†’
228â†’```kotlin
229â†’// import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
230â†’
231â†’println(DateTimeFormat.formatAsKotlinBuilderDsl(DateTimeComponents.Format {
232â†’    byUnicodePattern("uuuu-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss[.SSS]Z")
233â†’}))
234â†’
235â†’// will print:
236â†’/*
237â†’date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO)
238â†’char('T')
239â†’hour()
240â†’char(':')
241â†’minute()
242â†’char(':')
243â†’second()
244â†’alternativeParsing({
245â†’}) {
246â†’    char('.')
247â†’    secondFraction(3)
248â†’}
249â†’offset(UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS)
250â†’ */
251â†’```
252â†’
253â†’When your format string is not constant, with the `FormatStringsInDatetimeFormats` opt-in,
254â†’you can use the format without converting it to Kotlin code beforehand:
255â†’
256â†’```kotlin
257â†’val formatPattern = "yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss[.SSS]"
258â†’
259â†’@OptIn(FormatStringsInDatetimeFormats::class)
260â†’val dateTimeFormat = LocalDateTime.Format {
261â†’    byUnicodePattern(formatPattern)
262â†’}
263â†’
264â†’dateTimeFormat.parse("2023-12-24T23:59:59")
265â†’```
266â†’
267â†’### Parsing and formatting partial, compound or out-of-bounds data
268â†’
269â†’Sometimes, the required string format doesn't fully correspond to any of the
270â†’classes `kotlinx-datetime` provides. In these cases, `DateTimeComponents`, a
271â†’collection of all date-time fields, can be used instead.
272â†’
273â†’```kotlin
274â†’// import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
275â†’
276â†’val yearMonth = DateTimeComponents.Format { year(); char('-'); monthNumber() }
277â†’    .parse("2024-01")
278â†’println(yearMonth.year)
279â†’println(yearMonth.monthNumber)
280â†’
281â†’val dateTimeOffset = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET
282â†’    .parse("2023-01-07T23:16:15.53+02:00")
283â†’println(dateTimeOffset.toUtcOffset()) // +02:00
284â†’println(dateTimeOffset.toLocalDateTime()) // 2023-01-07T23:16:15.53
285â†’```
286â†’
287â†’Occasionally, one can encounter strings where the values are slightly off:
288â†’for example, `23:59:60`, where `60` is an invalid value for the second.
289â†’`DateTimeComponents` allows parsing such values as well and then mutating them
290â†’before conversion.
291â†’
292â†’```kotlin
293â†’val time = DateTimeComponents.Format { time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO) }
294â†’    .parse("23:59:60").apply {
295â†’        if (second == 60) second = 59
296â†’    }.toLocalTime()
297â†’println(time) // 23:59:59
298â†’```
299â†’
300â†’Because `DateTimeComponents` is provided specifically for parsing and
301â†’formatting, there is no way to construct it normally. If one needs to format
302â†’partial, complex or out-of-bounds data, the `format` function allows building
303â†’`DateTimeComponents` specifically for formatting it:
304â†’
305â†’```kotlin
306â†’DateTimeComponents.Formats.RFC_1123.format {
307â†’    // the receiver of this lambda is DateTimeComponents
308â†’    setDate(LocalDate(2023, 1, 7))
309â†’    hour = 23
310â†’    minute = 59
311â†’    second = 60
312â†’    setOffset(UtcOffset(hours = 2))
313â†’} // Sat, 7 Jan 2023 23:59:60 +0200
314â†’```
315â†’
316â†’### Instant arithmetic
317â†’
318â†’```kotlin
319â†’val now = Clock.System.now()
320â†’val instantInThePast: Instant = Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:00:00Z")
321â†’val durationSinceThen: Duration = now - instantInThePast
322â†’val equidistantInstantInTheFuture: Instant = now + durationSinceThen
323â†’```
324â†’
325â†’`Duration` is a type from the experimental `kotlin.time` package in the Kotlin standard library.
326â†’This type holds the amount of time that can be represented in different time units: from nanoseconds to 24H days.
327â†’
328â†’To get the calendar difference between two instants you can use the `Instant.periodUntil(Instant, TimeZone)` function.
329â†’
330â†’```kotlin
331â†’val period: DateTimePeriod = instantInThePast.periodUntil(Clock.System.now(), TimeZone.UTC)
332â†’```
333â†’
334â†’A `DateTimePeriod` represents a difference between two particular moments as a sum of calendar components, 
335â†’like "2 years, 3 months, 10 days, and 22 hours".
336â†’
337â†’The difference can be calculated as an integer amount of specified date or time units:
338â†’
339â†’```kotlin
340â†’val diffInMonths = instantInThePast.until(Clock.System.now(), DateTimeUnit.MONTH, TimeZone.UTC)
341â†’```
342â†’There are also shortcuts `yearsUntil(...)`, `monthsUntil(...)`, and `daysUntil(...)`.
343â†’
344â†’A particular amount of date/time units or a date/time period can be added to an `Instant` with the `plus` function:
345â†’
346â†’```kotlin
347â†’val now = Clock.System.now()
348â†’val systemTZ = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
349â†’val tomorrow = now.plus(2, DateTimeUnit.DAY, systemTZ)
350â†’val threeYearsAndAMonthLater = now.plus(DateTimePeriod(years = 3, months = 1), systemTZ)
351â†’```
352â†’
353â†’Note that `plus` and `...until` operations require a `TimeZone` as a parameter because the calendar interval between 
354â†’two particular instants can be different, when calculated in different time zones.
355â†’
356â†’### Date arithmetic
357â†’
358â†’Similar operations with date units are provided for `LocalDate` type:
359â†’
360â†’- `LocalDate.plus(number, DateTimeUnit.DateBased)`
361â†’- `LocalDate.plus(DatePeriod)`
362â†’- `LocalDate.until(LocalDate, DateTimeUnit.DateBased)` and the shortcuts `yearsUntil`, `monthUntil`, `daysUntil`
363â†’- `LocalDate.periodUntil(LocalDate): DatePeriod` and `LocalDate.minus(LocalDate): DatePeriod`
364â†’
365â†’Notice that, instead of the general `DateTimeUnit` and `DateTimePeriod` types, we're using their subtypes
366â†’`DateTimeUnit.DateBased` and `DatePeriod` respectively. This allows preventing the situations when
367â†’time components are being added to a date at compile time.
368â†’
369â†’### Date + time arithmetic
370â†’
371â†’Arithmetic on `LocalDateTime` is intentionally omitted. The reason for this is that the presence of daylight saving time
372â†’transitions (changing from standard time to daylight saving time and back) causes `LocalDateTime` arithmetic to be
373â†’ill-defined. For example, consider time gaps (or, as [`dst` tag wiki on Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/tags/dst/info)
374â†’calls them, "spring forward" transitions), that is, ranges of date + time combinations that never occur in a given
375â†’time zone due to clocks moving forward. If we allowed `LocalDateTime` arithmetic that ignored time zones, then it
376â†’could result in `LocalDateTime` instances that are inside a time gap and are invalid in the implied time zone.
377â†’
378â†’Therefore, the recommended way to use a `LocalDateTime` is to treat it as a representation of an `Instant`,
379â†’perform all the required arithmetic on `Instant` values, and only convert to `LocalDateTime` when a human-readable
380â†’representation is needed.
381â†’
382â†’```kotlin
383â†’val timeZone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
384â†’val localDateTime = LocalDateTime.parse("2021-03-27T02:16:20")
385â†’val instant = localDateTime.toInstant(timeZone)
386â†’
387â†’val instantOneDayLater = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
388â†’val localDateTimeOneDayLater = instantOneDayLater.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
389â†’// 2021-03-28T03:16:20, as 02:16:20 that day is in a time gap
390â†’
391â†’val instantTwoDaysLater = instant.plus(2, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
392â†’val localDateTimeTwoDaysLater = instantTwoDaysLater.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
393â†’// 2021-03-29T02:16:20
394â†’```
395â†’
396â†’## Implementation
397â†’
398â†’The implementation of date/time types, such as `Instant`, `LocalDateTime`, `TimeZone` and so on, relies on:
399â†’
400â†’- in JVM: [`java.time`](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/package-summary.html) API;
401â†’- in Js and Wasm-Js: [`js-joda`](https://js-joda.github.io/js-joda/) library;
402â†’- in Native: based on the [ThreeTen backport project](https://www.threeten.org/threetenbp/)
403â†’  - time zone support is provided by [date](https://github.com/HowardHinnant/date/) C++ library;
404â†’
405â†’## Known/open issues, work TBD
406â†’
407â†’- [x] Some kind of `Clock` interface is needed as a pluggable replacement for `Instant.now()`.
408â†’- [ ] Flexible locale-neutral parsing and formatting facilities are needed to support various date/time interchange
409â†’  formats that are used in practice (in particular, various RFCs).
410â†’
411â†’## Using in your projects
412â†’
413â†’> Note that the library is experimental, and the API is subject to change.
414â†’
415â†’The library is published to Maven Central.
416â†’
417â†’The library is compatible with the Kotlin Standard Library not lower than `1.9.0`.
418â†’
419â†’If you target Android devices running **below API 26**, you need to use Android Gradle plugin 4.0 or newer 
420â†’and enable [core library desugaring](https://developer.android.com/studio/write/java8-support#library-desugaring).
421â†’
422â†’### Gradle
423â†’
424â†’- Add the Maven Central repository if it is not already there:
425â†’
426â†’```kotlin
427â†’repositories {
428â†’    mavenCentral()
429â†’}
430â†’```
431â†’
432â†’- In multiplatform projects, add a dependency to the commonMain source set dependencies
433â†’```kotlin
434â†’kotlin {
435â†’    sourceSets {
436â†’        commonMain {
437â†’             dependencies {
438â†’                 implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-datetime:0.6.0-RC.2")
439â†’             }
440â†’        }
441â†’    }
442â†’}
443â†’```
444â†’
445â†’- To use the library in a single-platform project, add a dependency to the dependencies block.
446â†’
447â†’```groovy
448â†’dependencies {
449â†’    implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-datetime:0.6.0-RC.2")
450â†’}
451â†’```
452â†’
453â†’#### Note about time zones in JS
454â†’
455â†’By default, there's only one time zone available in Kotlin/JS: the `SYSTEM` time zone with a fixed offset.
456â†’
457â†’If you want to use all time zones in Kotlin/JS platform, you need to add the following npm dependency:
458â†’
459â†’```kotlin
460â†’kotlin {
461â†’    sourceSets {
462â†’        val jsMain by getting {
463â†’            dependencies {
464â†’                implementation(npm("@js-joda/timezone", "2.3.0"))
465â†’            }
466â†’        }
467â†’    }
468â†’}
469â†’```
470â†’
471â†’and after that add the following initialization code in your project:
472â†’
473â†’```kotlin
474â†’@JsModule("@js-joda/timezone")
475â†’@JsNonModule
476â†’external object JsJodaTimeZoneModule
477â†’
478â†’private val jsJodaTz = JsJodaTimeZoneModule
479â†’```
480â†’
481â†’### Maven
482â†’
483â†’Add a dependency to the `<dependencies>` element. Note that you need to use the platform-specific `-jvm` artifact in Maven.
484â†’
485â†’```xml
486â†’<dependency>
487â†’    <groupId>org.jetbrains.kotlinx</groupId>
488â†’    <artifactId>kotlinx-datetime-jvm</artifactId>
489â†’    <version>0.6.0-RC.2</version>
490â†’</dependency>
491â†’```
492â†’
493â†’## Building
494â†’
495â†’The project requires JDK 8 to build classes and to run tests. 
496â†’Gradle will try to find it among the installed JDKs or [provision](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/toolchains.html#sec:provisioning) it automatically if it couldn't be found.
497â†’The path to JDK 8 can be additionally specified with the environment variable `JDK_8`. 
498â†’For local builds, you can use a later version of JDK if you don't have that 
499â†’version installed. Specify the version of this JDK with the `java.mainToolchainVersion` Gradle property.
500â†’
501â†’After that, the project can be opened in IDEA and built with Gradle.
502â†’
503â†’For building and running benchmarks, see [README.md](benchmarks/README.md)
504â†’

 6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
 7â†’
 8â†’import kotlin.time.*
 9â†’
10â†’/**
11â†’ * A source of [Instant] values.
12â†’ *
13â†’ * See [Clock.System][Clock.System] for the clock instance that queries the operating system.
14â†’ */
15â†’public interface Clock {
16â†’    /**
17â†’     * Returns the [Instant] corresponding to the current time, according to this clock.
18â†’     */
19â†’    public fun now(): Instant
20â†’
21â†’    /**
22â†’     * The [Clock] instance that queries the operating system as its source of knowledge of time.
23â†’     */
24â†’    public object System : Clock {
25â†’        override fun now(): Instant = @Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR") Instant.now()
26â†’    }
27â†’
28â†’    public companion object {
29â†’
30â†’    }
31â†’}
32â†’
33â†’/**
34â†’ * Returns the current date at the given [time zone][timeZone], according to [this Clock][this].
35â†’ */
36â†’public fun Clock.todayIn(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate =
37â†’    now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone).date
38â†’
39â†’/**
40â†’ * Returns a [TimeSource] that uses this [Clock] to mark a time instant and to find the amount of time elapsed since that mark.
41â†’ */
42â†’@ExperimentalTime
43â†’public fun Clock.asTimeSource(): TimeSource.WithComparableMarks = object : TimeSource.WithComparableMarks {
44â†’    override fun markNow(): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(now(), this@asTimeSource)
45â†’}
46â†’
47â†’@ExperimentalTime
48â†’private class InstantTimeMark(private val instant: Instant, private val clock: Clock) : ComparableTimeMark {
49â†’    override fun elapsedNow(): Duration = saturatingDiff(clock.now(), instant)
50â†’
51â†’    override fun plus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(duration), clock)
52â†’    override fun minus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(-duration), clock)
53â†’
54â†’    override fun minus(other: ComparableTimeMark): Duration {
55â†’        if (other !is InstantTimeMark || other.clock != this.clock) {
56â†’            throw IllegalArgumentException("Subtracting or comparing time marks from different time sources is not possible: $this and $other")
57â†’        }
58â†’        return saturatingDiff(this.instant, other.instant)
59â†’    }
60â†’
61â†’    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
62â†’        return other is InstantTimeMark && this.clock == other.clock && this.instant == other.instant
63â†’    }
64â†’
65â†’    override fun hashCode(): Int = instant.hashCode()
66â†’
67â†’    override fun toString(): String = "InstantTimeMark($instant, $clock)"
68â†’
69â†’    private fun Instant.isSaturated() = this == Instant.MAX || this == Instant.MIN
70â†’    private fun Instant.saturatingAdd(duration: Duration): Instant {
71â†’        if (isSaturated()) {
72â†’            if (duration.isInfinite() && duration.isPositive() != this.isDistantFuture) {
73â†’                throw IllegalArgumentException("Summing infinities of different signs")
74â†’            }
75â†’            return this
76â†’        }
77â†’        return this + duration
78â†’    }
79â†’    private fun saturatingDiff(instant1: Instant, instant2: Instant): Duration = when {
80â†’        instant1 == instant2 ->
81â†’            Duration.ZERO
82â†’        instant1.isSaturated() || instant2.isSaturated() ->
83â†’            (instant1 - instant2) * Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY
84â†’        else ->
85â†’            instant1 - instant2
86â†’    }
87â†’}
88â†’
89â†’@Deprecated("Use Clock.todayIn instead", ReplaceWith("this.todayIn(timeZone)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
90â†’public fun Clock.todayAt(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate = todayIn(timeZone)

 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A moment in time.
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 18â†’ * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 19â†’ * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 20â†’ * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 21â†’ * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 22â†’ * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 23â†’ *
 24â†’ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 25â†’ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 26â†’ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 29â†’ * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 30â†’ *   moment as the platform sees it.
 31â†’ * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 32â†’ * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 33â†’ *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 34â†’ */
 35â†’@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 36â†’public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
 37â†’
 38â†’    /**
 39â†’     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
 40â†’     *
 41â†’     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
 42â†’     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
 43â†’     *
 44â†’     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
 45â†’     *
 46â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 47â†’     */
 48â†’    public val epochSeconds: Long
 49â†’
 50â†’    /**
 51â†’     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
 52â†’     *
 53â†’     * The value is always positive and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.
 54â†’     *
 55â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 56â†’     */
 57â†’    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
 58â†’
 59â†’    /**
 60â†’     * Returns the number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
 61â†’     *
 62â†’     * Any fractional part of millisecond is rounded down to the whole number of milliseconds.
 63â†’     *
 64â†’     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.

 74â†’     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 75â†’     *
 76â†’     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 77â†’     */
 78â†’    public operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant
 79â†’
 80â†’    /**
 81â†’     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 84â†’     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 85â†’     *
 86â†’     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 87â†’     */
 88â†’    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant

 90â†’    // questionable
 91â†’    /**
 92â†’     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
 93â†’     *
 94â†’     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
 95â†’     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
 96â†’     *

 98â†’     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
 99â†’     */
100â†’    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration
101â†’
102â†’    /**
103â†’     * Compares `this` instant with the [other] instant.
104â†’     * Returns zero if this instant represents the same moment as the other (i.e. equal to other),
105â†’     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,
106â†’     * and a positive number if this instant is later than the other.
107â†’     */
108â†’    public override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int
109â†’
110â†’    /**
111â†’     * Converts this instant to the ISO-8601 string representation.
112â†’     *
113â†’     * The representation uses the UTC-SLS time scale, instead of UTC.
114â†’     * In practice, this means that leap second handling will not be readjusted to the UTC.
115â†’     * Leap seconds will not be added or skipped, so it is impossible to acquire a string
116â†’     * where the component for seconds is 60, and for any day, it's possible to observe 23:59:59.
117â†’     *
118â†’     * @see Instant.parse
119â†’     * @see DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET for a very similar format. The difference is that
120â†’     * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] will not add trailing zeros for readability to the
121â†’     * fractional part of the second.
122â†’     */
123â†’    public override fun toString(): String
124â†’
125â†’
126â†’    public companion object {
127â†’        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
128â†’        public fun now(): Instant
129â†’
130â†’        /**
131â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is [epochMilliseconds] number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
132â†’         *
133â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
134â†’         *

306â†’/**
307â†’ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
308â†’ *
309â†’ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
310â†’ * the time components are zero.
311â†’ *
312â†’ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
313â†’ * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
314â†’ */
315â†’@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
316â†’public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
317â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
318â†’    override val days: Int,
319â†’) : DateTimePeriod() {
320â†’    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
321â†’    // avoiding excessive computations
322â†’    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
323â†’    override val hours: Int get() = 0
324â†’
325â†’    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
326â†’    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
327â†’
328â†’    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
329â†’    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
330â†’
331â†’    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
332â†’    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
333â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0

335â†’    public companion object {
336â†’        /**
337â†’         * Parses the ISO-8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod].
338â†’         *
339â†’         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
340â†’         * zero.
341â†’         *
342â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
343â†’         * or any time components are not zero.
344â†’         *
345â†’         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
346â†’         */
347â†’        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
348â†’            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
349â†’                is DatePeriod -> period
350â†’                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
351â†’            }
352â†’    }

361â†’private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
362â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
363â†’    override val days: Int,
364â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
365â†’) : DateTimePeriod()

367â†’// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
368â†’private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
369â†’    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
370â†’        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
371â†’        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
372â†’    }

374â†’private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
375â†’    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
376â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
377â†’    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
378â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
379â†’    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
380â†’    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
381â†’    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
382â†’    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
383â†’    return try {
384â†’        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
385â†’    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
386â†’        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
387â†’    }
388â†’}

390â†’internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
391â†’    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
392â†’        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
393â†’    else
394â†’        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
